title: 2021 World Figure Skating Championships

infobox.value: were held from March 22 to 28, 2021, at the [Ericsson Globe](/wiki/Ericsson_Globe "Ericsson Globe") in [Stockholm, Sweden](/wiki/Stockholm,_Sweden "Stockholm, Sweden"). Sanctioned by the [International Skating Union](/wiki/International_Skating_Union "International Skating Union") (ISU), the [World Championships](/wiki/World_Figure_Skating_Championships "World Figure Skating Championships") are considered the most prestigious event in figure skating. Medals were awarded in [men's singles, women's singles](/wiki/Single_skating "Single skating"), [pair skating](/wiki/Pair_skating "Pair skating"), and [ice dance](/wiki/Ice_dance "Ice dance"). The competition determined the entry quotas for each skating federation at the [2022 World Championships](/wiki/2022_World_Figure_Skating_Championships "2022 World Figure Skating Championships") and was the first qualifying event for the [2022 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_2022_Winter_Olympics "Figure skating at the 2022 Winter Olympics").

The **2021 World Figure Skating Championships** were held from March 22 to 28, 2021, at the [Ericsson Globe](/wiki/Ericsson_Globe "Ericsson Globe") in [Stockholm, Sweden](/wiki/Stockholm,_Sweden "Stockholm, Sweden"). Sanctioned by the [International Skating Union](/wiki/International_Skating_Union "International Skating Union") (ISU), the [World Championships](/wiki/World_Figure_Skating_Championships "World Figure Skating Championships") are considered the most prestigious event in figure skating. Medals were awarded in [men's singles, women's singles](/wiki/Single_skating "Single skating"), [pair skating](/wiki/Pair_skating "Pair skating"), and [ice dance](/wiki/Ice_dance "Ice dance"). The competition determined the entry quotas for each skating federation at the [2022 World Championships](/wiki/2022_World_Figure_Skating_Championships "2022 World Figure Skating Championships") and was the first qualifying event for the [2022 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_2022_Winter_Olympics "Figure skating at the 2022 Winter Olympics").

The World Championships were the only [ISU Championship](/wiki/ISU_Figure_Skating_Championships "ISU Figure Skating Championships") event held during the [2020–21 season](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_figure_skating_season "2020–21 figure skating season"), while the [European Championships](/wiki/2021_European_Figure_Skating_Championships "2021 European Figure Skating Championships"), [Four Continents Championships](/wiki/2021_Four_Continents_Figure_Skating_Championships "2021 Four Continents Figure Skating Championships"), and [World Junior Championships](/wiki/2021_World_Junior_Figure_Skating_Championships "2021 World Junior Figure Skating Championships") were all cancelled due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/Impact_of_the_COVID-19_pandemic_on_sports#Figure_skating "Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on sports"). The event was [held without spectators](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Sweden#Sports "COVID-19 pandemic in Sweden"), while attendees at the competition remained in a [bubble](/wiki/Bio-secure_bubble "Bio-secure bubble") – that is, in a cluster made up exclusively of individuals who have been thoroughly tested and were unlikely to spread infection – throughout the event.

[Nathan Chen](/wiki/Nathan_Chen "Nathan Chen") of the United States won the men's event, [Anna Shcherbakova](/wiki/Anna_Shcherbakova "Anna Shcherbakova") of Russia won the women's event, [Anastasia Mishina](/wiki/Anastasia_Mishina "Anastasia Mishina") and [Aleksandr Galliamov](/wiki/Aleksandr_Galliamov "Aleksandr Galliamov") of Russia won the pairs event, and [Victoria Sinitsina](/wiki/Victoria_Sinitsina "Victoria Sinitsina") and [Nikita Katsalapov](/wiki/Nikita_Katsalapov "Nikita Katsalapov") of Russia won the ice dance event. Because of a ruling by the [Court of Arbitration for Sport](/wiki/Court_of_Arbitration_for_Sport "Court of Arbitration for Sport"), all Russian skaters competed as representatives of the [Figure Skating Federation of Russia](/wiki/Figure_Skating_Federation_of_Russia "Figure Skating Federation of Russia") (FSR).

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2021_World_Figure_Skating_Championships&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background")]

The [World Figure Skating Championships](/wiki/World_Figure_Skating_Championships "World Figure Skating Championships") are considered the most prestigious event in figure skating.[[1]](#cite_note-1) The 2021 World Championships were held from March 22 to 28, 2021, at the [Ericsson Globe](/wiki/Ericsson_Globe "Ericsson Globe") in [Stockholm, Sweden](/wiki/Stockholm,_Sweden "Stockholm, Sweden").[[2]](#cite_note-Stockholm-Announcement-2) The 2021 World Championships were the only [ISU Championship](/wiki/ISU_Figure_Skating_Championships "ISU Figure Skating Championships") event held during the [2020–21 season](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_figure_skating_season "2020–21 figure skating season") owing to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/Impact_of_the_COVID-19_pandemic_on_sports#Figure_skating "Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on sports").[[3]](#cite_note-WSJ210324-3)

### Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2021_World_Figure_Skating_Championships&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic")]

The [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic") began with an [outbreak](/wiki/Disease_outbreak "Disease outbreak") in China in December 2019. Soon after, it spread to other areas of Asia and [then worldwide](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_by_country_and_territory "COVID-19 pandemic by country and territory") in early 2020. The [World Health Organization](/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization") declared the outbreak a [public health emergency of international concern](/wiki/Public_health_emergency_of_international_concern "Public health emergency of international concern") on January 30, 2020, and assessed the outbreak as having become a [pandemic](/wiki/Pandemic "Pandemic") on March 11, 2020.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

[COVID‑19 transmission](/wiki/Transmission_of_COVID-19 "Transmission of COVID-19") occurs when infectious particles are breathed in or come into contact with the eyes, nose, or mouth. The risk is highest when people are in close proximity, but small [airborne](/wiki/Airborne_transmission "Airborne transmission") particles containing the virus can remain suspended in the air and travel over long distances, particularly indoors. People can remain contagious for up to twenty days and can spread the virus even if they do not develop symptoms themselves.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Several [COVID-19 vaccines](/wiki/COVID-19_vaccine "COVID-19 vaccine") were approved and distributed, many of which initiated [mass vaccination campaigns](/wiki/Deployment_of_COVID-19_vaccines "Deployment of COVID-19 vaccines"). Other [preventive measures](/wiki/Public_health_mitigation_of_COVID-19 "Public health mitigation of COVID-19") included [social distancing](/wiki/Social_distancing "Social distancing"), [quarantining](/wiki/Quarantine "Quarantine"), ventilation of indoor spaces, [use of face masks or coverings](/wiki/Face_masks_during_the_COVID-19_pandemic "Face masks during the COVID-19 pandemic") in public, covering coughs and sneezes, and [hand washing](/wiki/Hand_washing "Hand washing").[[6]](#cite_note-6) One [testing method](/wiki/COVID-19_testing "COVID-19 testing") to see if a person was infected with COVID-19 was real-time [reverse transcription polymerase chain reaction](/wiki/Reverse_transcription_polymerase_chain_reaction "Reverse transcription polymerase chain reaction") (PCR),[[7]](#cite_note-7) which was used at the 2021 World Championships.[[8]](#cite_note-NBC210316-8)

### Scheduling

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2021_World_Figure_Skating_Championships&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Scheduling")]

The pandemic caused significant disruption to the [2020–21 figure skating season](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_figure_skating_season "2020–21 figure skating season"). The complete [Junior Grand Prix](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_ISU_Junior_Grand_Prix "2020–21 ISU Junior Grand Prix") series was cancelled on July 20, 2020.[[9]](#cite_note-ISU-200720-9) On October 16, 2020, the [International Skating Union](/wiki/International_Skating_Union "International Skating Union") (ISU) announced the cancellation of the [2021 Four Continents Figure Skating Championships](/wiki/2021_Four_Continents_Figure_Skating_Championships "2021 Four Continents Figure Skating Championships"),[[10]](#cite_note-10) and on November 24, 2020, the [2021 World Junior Figure Skating Championships](/wiki/2021_World_Junior_Figure_Skating_Championships "2021 World Junior Figure Skating Championships").[[11]](#cite_note-11) The [2020–21 Grand Prix of Figure Skating Final](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_Grand_Prix_of_Figure_Skating_Final "2020–21 Grand Prix of Figure Skating Final"), [2021 European Figure Skating Championships](/wiki/2021_European_Figure_Skating_Championships "2021 European Figure Skating Championships"), and [2021 World Synchronized Skating Championships](/wiki/ISU_World_Synchronized_Skating_Championships "ISU World Synchronized Skating Championships") were definitively cancelled on December 10, 2020.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Despite these cancellations, ISU Vice President Alexander Lakernik stated that the ISU would make "every effort" to hold the World Championships.[[13]](#cite_note-13) A Swedish government ban which limited entrants in international competitions hosted by Sweden to only those from the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") was lifted on November 20, 2020.[[14]](#cite_note-14) However, on December 22, 2020, Skate Sweden cancelled all domestic championships for the remainder of the 2020–21 season in compliance with [Public Health Agency of Sweden](/wiki/Public_Health_Agency_of_Sweden "Public Health Agency of Sweden") guidelines.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

During its January 28 meeting, the ISU Council affirmed that the World Championships would proceed as scheduled.[[16]](#cite_note-TASS210128-16) The council also announced that the status of the World Championships as a [qualifying event for the 2022 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_2022_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Qualification "Figure skating at the 2022 Winter Olympics – Qualification") would be reevaluated based on the entries received by the ISU as of the March 1, 2021, deadline.[[17]](#cite_note-ISU2367-17) On March 4, the ISU affirmed that the Olympic qualifying process would proceed as previously announced, as nearly all [ISU member nations](/wiki/International_figure_skating "International figure skating") with qualified skaters had confirmed entry to the 2021 World Championships.[[18]](#cite_note-ISU2380-18) The 2021 World Figure Skating Championships were ultimately held from March 22 to 28, 2021, at the [Ericsson Globe](/wiki/Ericsson_Globe "Ericsson Globe") in [Stockholm](/wiki/Stockholm,_Sweden "Stockholm, Sweden").[[2]](#cite_note-Stockholm-Announcement-2)

### ISU member nations' responses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2021_World_Figure_Skating_Championships&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: ISU member nations' responses")]

In November 2020, [Skate Canada](/wiki/Skate_Canada "Skate Canada") stated that if the 2021 World Championships continued as planned, it might not participate due to insufficient training time and preparation on behalf of its skaters, as well as the mandatory fourteen-day quarantine upon return to Canada.[[19]](#cite_note-CBC11262020-19) Despite the cancellation of the [2021 Canadian Championships](/wiki/2021_Canadian_Figure_Skating_Championships "2021 Canadian Figure Skating Championships") and a lack of competitive opportunities for its skaters, Skate Canada announced its selection criteria for naming a World Championship team in January 2021.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

The [Japan Skating Federation](/wiki/Japan_Skating_Federation "Japan Skating Federation") withdrew its delegation from the [2021 World Short Track Speed Skating Championships](/wiki/2021_World_Short_Track_Speed_Skating_Championships "2021 World Short Track Speed Skating Championships") scheduled for early March,[[21]](#cite_note-21) calling into question the participation of its figure skaters at the 2021 World Championships.[[22]](#cite_note-22) The Japan Skating Federation eventually sent skaters to the World Championships due to it being an Olympic qualifying event. The Japanese delegation arrived at the competition throughout the weekend of March 20–22, with all athletes receiving a negative PCR test upon arrival.[[23]](#cite_note-JSF210322-23)

### Skaters' responses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2021_World_Figure_Skating_Championships&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Skaters' responses")]

Skaters arrived at the event with varying levels of preparation due to their respective governments' and home federations' responses to the pandemic.[[24]](#cite_note-WSJ201227-24)[[25]](#cite_note-CBC210322-25) Multiple skaters had been unable to train with their primary coaches due to travel or [visa](/wiki/Travel_visa "Travel visa") restrictions and spent the season in temporary training situations, either on their own or with different coaches.[[26]](#cite_note-OC201224-26)[[27]](#cite_note-MT210309-27) Many pairs and dance teams were also unable to have physical contact when they could train on the ice due to their nations' respective restrictions, further limiting their preparedness.[[19]](#cite_note-CBC11262020-19)

In January 2021, [Gabriella Papadakis](/wiki/Gabriella_Papadakis "Gabriella Papadakis") and [Guillaume Cizeron](/wiki/Guillaume_Cizeron "Guillaume Cizeron") of France, the defending world champions in [ice dance](/wiki/Ice_dance "Ice dance"), announced that they would not compete at the World Championships; they planned to focus instead on the [2022 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_2022_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Ice_dance "Figure skating at the 2022 Winter Olympics – Ice dance").[[28]](#cite_note-28) [Nathan Chen](/wiki/Nathan_Chen "Nathan Chen") of the United States, the defending world champion in the men's event, stated that while he was grateful for the opportunity to compete, he had concerns about COVID-19 safety protocols, citing the fourteen-hour flight to Sweden as his biggest concern.[[8]](#cite_note-NBC210316-8) Pairs skater [Kirsten Moore-Towers](/wiki/Kirsten_Moore-Towers "Kirsten Moore-Towers") of Canada described "anxiousness over the unknown".[[29]](#cite_note-CBC210316-29) Some skaters, however, felt that adequate precautions were being taken and they would likely be safe at the event. [Emily Bausback](/wiki/Emily_Bausback "Emily Bausback") of Canada stated: "I knew we wouldn't be going if it wasn't safe... I feel comfortable with the situation."[[30]](#cite_note-TP210226-30) [Elizaveta Tuktamysheva](/wiki/Elizaveta_Tuktamysheva "Elizaveta Tuktamysheva") of Russia expressed no hesitation about competing at the World Championships, having already had COVID-19.[[31]](#cite_note-OC210317-31)

### Criticism and concerns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2021_World_Figure_Skating_Championships&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Criticism and concerns")]

During the months preceding the arrival of skaters and officials in Stockholm, a petition calling for the ISU to implement stricter quarantine measures and a tighter bubble, a cluster made up exclusively of individuals who have been thoroughly tested and unlikely to spread infection,[[32]](#cite_note-32) garnered thousands of signatures.[[33]](#cite_note-ITG210317-33) The [Ericsson Globe](/wiki/Ericsson_Globe "Ericsson Globe") (the site of the competition arena), two practice arenas, and a dedicated hotel for skaters and officials were interconnected and allowed passage from one place to another via indoor walkways. A negative [PCR test](/wiki/PCR_testing_procedure "PCR testing procedure") was required within forty-eight hours of arrival in Sweden, with a series of follow-up tests scheduled afterward. However, there was no [quarantine](/wiki/Quarantine "Quarantine") requirement prior to the start of the competition.[[8]](#cite_note-NBC210316-8)

The attendance of skaters representing the [Figure Skating Federation of Russia](/wiki/Figure_Skating_Federation_of_Russia "Figure Skating Federation of Russia") (FSR) drew particular scrutiny, as the federation was accused of holding several domestic and international events in violation of the ISU's COVID-19 safety protocols.[[24]](#cite_note-WSJ201227-24) Despite some precautionary measures being taken, the organizers of the [2020 Rostelecom Cup](/wiki/2020_Rostelecom_Cup "2020 Rostelecom Cup"), as with other Russian domestic competitions during the season, came under criticism for allowing a large audience and an insufficient enforcement of mandates regarding [social distancing](/wiki/Social_distancing "Social distancing") or [proper use of face masks](/wiki/Face_masks_during_the_COVID-19_pandemic "Face masks during the COVID-19 pandemic"). A focal point of criticism was the decision to hold the traditional post-competition banquet, where many skaters were documented not wearing masks or socially distancing at all, as well as sharing food from a communal buffet.[[34]](#cite_note-RS2012042-34) Additionally, a large number of Russian skaters had contracted COVID-19 during the season, including [Elizaveta Tuktamysheva](/wiki/Elizaveta_Tuktamysheva "Elizaveta Tuktamysheva"), [Victoria Sinitsina](/wiki/Victoria_Sinitsina "Victoria Sinitsina"), and [Nikita Katsalapov](/wiki/Nikita_Katsalapov "Nikita Katsalapov").[[35]](#cite_note-OC201203-35)[[36]](#cite_note-TVZ201222-36) Nevertheless, all members of the Russian delegation tested negative during the initial round of testing administered upon arrival in Stockholm.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

### During the competition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2021_World_Figure_Skating_Championships&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: During the competition")]

[Victoria, Crown Princess of Sweden](/wiki/Victoria,_Crown_Princess_of_Sweden "Victoria, Crown Princess of Sweden"), had been scheduled to participate in the opening ceremony before she and her husband, [Prince Daniel](/wiki/Prince_Daniel,_Duke_of_V%C3%A4sterg%C3%B6tland "Prince Daniel, Duke of Västergötland"), contracted COVID-19 a few weeks prior to the event.[[38]](#cite_note-SS210311-38) She instead appeared in a video message from [Haga Palace](/wiki/Haga_Palace "Haga Palace") to welcome the athletes.[[39]](#cite_note-people210325-39)

Delegations began arriving on March 20 and 21, and members underwent the required multiple tests and quarantine until a negative result was returned. Attendees were not eligible for accreditation until they passed a second PCR test. After receiving accreditation, limited testing was conducted, and attendees were only required to fill out daily symptom questionnaires and undergo temperature checks.[[3]](#cite_note-WSJ210324-3)

Attendees at the competition remained in a bubble throughout the event.[[2]](#cite_note-Stockholm-Announcement-2) On March 22, the ISU announced that one positive case had been found during one of the initial rounds of testing and that [contact tracing](/wiki/Contact_tracing "Contact tracing") procedures were being undertaken.[[40]](#cite_note-ISU210322-40) The skater with the positive case, later revealed to be [Viktoriia Safonova](/wiki/Viktoriia_Safonova "Viktoriia Safonova") of Belarus, was quarantined and not allowed to compete. Safonova's coach subsequently tested negative in a second round of testing, and Safonova, who had traveled to Stockholm from a different location, had had no contact with the other members of the Belarusian team or their coaches.[[41]](#cite_note-safonova-wd-41) On March 24, the ISU announced that a second positive case had been found during the initial round of testing. Without accreditation, neither skater with positive test results had been allowed into the competition bubble.[[42]](#cite_note-ISU210324-42)

On March 26, the ISU announced that a third positive case had been found, this time within the bubble.[[43]](#cite_note-ISU210326-43) After the event, [Simon Proulx-Sénécal](/wiki/Simon_Proulx-S%C3%A9n%C3%A9cal "Simon Proulx-Sénécal") of Armenia revealed that he had tested positive on March 25, the day before the ice dance competition was scheduled to start, and asked for a re-test prior to the [rhythm dance](/wiki/Rhythm_dance "Rhythm dance") segment on March 26. As the test result did not come back until after the rhythm dance had concluded, Proulx-Sénécal and his partner, [Tina Garabedian](/wiki/Tina_Garabedian "Tina Garabedian"), were not allowed to compete. The result came back as a second positive. Proulx-Sénécal expressed that he believed his first test was a [false positive](/wiki/False_positives_and_false_negatives "False positives and false negatives"), as his federation later received a written test result stating that his second test, taken the morning of the rhythm dance, was negative, not positive as initially conveyed to their team doctor.[[44]](#cite_note-Proulx-Sénécal-44) He subsequently tested negative twice more, once the day after the rhythm dance (March 27) and again upon returning to [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal") on March 29.[[45]](#cite_note-JQ210330-45)

In 2016, an [independent report](/wiki/McLaren_Report "McLaren Report") commissioned by the [World Anti-Doping Agency](/wiki/World_Anti-Doping_Agency "World Anti-Doping Agency") (WADA) confirmed allegations that the Russian Olympic team had been involved in a [state-sponsored doping program](/wiki/Doping_in_Russia "Doping in Russia"), active from at least late 2011 through August 2015.[[47]](#cite_note-NYT161209-47) On December 9, 2019, the WADA banned Russia from all international competitions after it found that data provided by the [Russian Anti-Doping Agency](/wiki/Russian_Anti-Doping_Agency "Russian Anti-Doping Agency") had been manipulated by Russian authorities in order to protect athletes involved in its state-sponsored doping scheme.[[48]](#cite_note-48) Under the ban imposed by the [Court of Arbitration for Sport](/wiki/Court_of_Arbitration_for_Sport "Court of Arbitration for Sport"), Russian athletes could not use the Russian flag or anthem in international competition and had to present themselves as "Neutral Athletes" or a "Neutral Team" at any world championships until December 16, 2022.[[49]](#cite_note-AP201217-49) Russian skaters competed under a modified flag of the [Figure Skating Federation of Russia](/wiki/Figure_Skating_Federation_of_Russia "Figure Skating Federation of Russia") and the name "FSR" at the 2021 World Championships. The ISU also approved the use of [Tchaikovsky](/wiki/Pyotr_Ilyich_Tchaikovsky "Pyotr Ilyich Tchaikovsky")'s [Piano Concerto No. 1](/wiki/Piano_Concerto_No._1_(Tchaikovsky) "Piano Concerto No. 1 (Tchaikovsky)") in lieu of the [Russian national anthem](/wiki/National_anthem_of_Russia "National anthem of Russia") during medal ceremonies.[[50]](#cite_note-Tchaikovsky-50)

## Required performance elements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2021_World_Figure_Skating_Championships&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Required performance elements")]

### Single skating

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2021_World_Figure_Skating_Championships&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Single skating")]

Women competing in [single skating](/wiki/Single_skating "Single skating") first performed their [short programs](/wiki/Short_program_(figure_skating) "Short program (figure skating)") on Wednesday, March 24, men performed theirs on Thursday, March 25.[[2]](#cite_note-Stockholm-Announcement-2) Lasting no more than 2 minutes 40 seconds,[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInternational_Skating_Union202175–76-68) the short program had to include the following elements:

**For men:** one [double or triple Axel](/wiki/Axel_jump "Axel jump"); one [triple or quadruple jump](/wiki/Figure_skating_jumps "Figure skating jumps"); one [jump combination](/wiki/Figure_skating_jumps "Figure skating jumps") consisting of a double jump and a triple jump, or a quadruple jump and a double jump or triple jump; one [flying spin](/wiki/Figure_skating_spins#Flying_spins "Figure skating spins"); one [camel spin](/wiki/Camel_spin "Camel spin") or [sit spin](/wiki/Sit_spin "Sit spin") with a change of foot; one [spin combination](/wiki/Figure_skating_spins#Spin_combinations "Figure skating spins") with a change of foot; and a [step sequence](/wiki/Step_sequence "Step sequence") using the full ice surface.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInternational_Skating_Union2021102–103-69)

**For women:** one double or triple Axel; one triple jump; one jump combination consisting of a double jump and a triple jump, or two triple jumps; one flying spin; one [layback spin](/wiki/Layback_spin "Layback spin"), [sideways leaning spin](/wiki/Upright_spin "Upright spin"), camel spin, or sit spin without a change of foot; one spin combination with a change of foot; and one step sequence using the full ice surface.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInternational_Skating_Union2021102–103-69)

The top 24 skaters after completion of the short program component of the competition moved on to the [free skating](/wiki/Free_skating "Free skating") component. Women performed their free skates on Friday, March 26; men performed their free skates on Saturday, March 27.[[2]](#cite_note-Stockholm-Announcement-2) The free skate performance for both men and women could last no more than 4 minutes,[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInternational_Skating_Union202175–76-68) and had to include the following: seven jump elements, of which one had to be an Axel-type jump; three spins, of which one had to be a spin combination, one had to be a flying spin, and one had to be a spin with only one position; a step sequence; and a [choreographic sequence](/wiki/Choreographic_sequence "Choreographic sequence").[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInternational_Skating_Union2021106–107-70)

### Pair skating

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2021_World_Figure_Skating_Championships&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Pair skating")]

Couples competing in [pair skating](/wiki/Pair_skating "Pair skating") performed their short programs on Wednesday, March 24.[[2]](#cite_note-Stockholm-Announcement-2) Lasting no more than 2 minutes 40 seconds,[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInternational_Skating_Union2021pp._75–76-71) it had to include the following elements: one [pair lift](/wiki/Figure_skating_lifts#Pair_lifts "Figure skating lifts"), one [twist lift](/wiki/Figure_skating_lifts#Twist_lifts "Figure skating lifts"), one [double or triple throw jump](/wiki/Pair_skating#Throw_jumps "Pair skating"), one [double or triple solo jump](/wiki/Pair_skating#Solo_jumps "Pair skating"), one [solo spin combination](/wiki/Figure_skating_spins#Solo_spin_combinations "Figure skating spins") with a change of foot, one [death spiral](/wiki/Death_spiral_(figure_skating) "Death spiral (figure skating)"), and a step sequence using the full ice surface.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInternational_Skating_Union2021111–112-72)

The top 20 couples after completion of the short program component moved on to the free skating component, which were performed on Thursday, March 25.[[2]](#cite_note-Stockholm-Announcement-2) The free skate performance could last no more than 4 minutes,[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInternational_Skating_Union202175–76-68) and had to include the following: three pair lifts, of which one had to be a twist lift; two different throw jumps; one solo jump; one jump combination or sequence; one [pair spin combination](/wiki/Figure_skating_spins#Pair_spin_combinations "Figure skating spins"); one death spiral; and a choreographic sequence.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInternational_Skating_Union2021115-73)

### Ice dance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2021_World_Figure_Skating_Championships&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Ice dance")]

See also: [Competition elements in ice dance](/wiki/Competition_elements_in_ice_dance "Competition elements in ice dance")

Couples competing in [ice dance](/wiki/Ice_dance "Ice dance") performed their [rhythm dances](/wiki/Rhythm_dance "Rhythm dance") on Friday, March 26.[[2]](#cite_note-Stockholm-Announcement-2) Lasting no more than 2 minutes 50 seconds,[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInternational_Skating_Union202175–76-68) the rhythm dance had to include the following elements: the [pattern dance](/wiki/Compulsory_dance "Compulsory dance"), the pattern dance step sequence, one [dance lift](/wiki/Competition_elements_in_ice_dance#Dance_lifts "Competition elements in ice dance"), one set of [sequential twizzles](/wiki/Competition_elements_in_ice_dance#Turn_sequences "Competition elements in ice dance"), and one [step sequence](/wiki/Competition_elements_in_ice_dance#Step_sequences "Competition elements in ice dance").[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInternational_Skating_Union2021139-74)[[75]](#cite_note-:0-75) The required pattern dance was the [Finnstep](/wiki/Compulsory_dance "Compulsory dance") – a fun, fast ballroom-style dance[[76]](#cite_note-76) – and music came from [musicals](/wiki/Musical_theatre "Musical theatre") or [operettas](/wiki/Operetta "Operetta").[[77]](#cite_note-ID-2021-77)

The top 20 couples, after completing the rhythm dance component of the competition moved on to the [free dance](/wiki/Free_dance_(ice_dance) "Free dance (ice dance)") component, which was held on Saturday, March 27.[[2]](#cite_note-Stockholm-Announcement-2) The free dance performance could last no longer than 4 minutes,[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInternational_Skating_Union202175–76-68) and had to include the following: three dance lifts, one [dance spin](/wiki/Competition_elements_in_ice_dance#Dance_spins "Competition elements in ice dance"), one set of [synchronized twizzles](/wiki/Competition_elements_in_ice_dance#Turn_sequences "Competition elements in ice dance"), one step sequence in hold, one step sequence while on one skate and not touching, and three [choreographic elements](/wiki/Competition_elements_in_ice_dance "Competition elements in ice dance"), of which one had to be a choreographic character step sequence.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInternational_Skating_Union2021141-78)[[75]](#cite_note-:0-75)

## Judging

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2021_World_Figure_Skating_Championships&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Judging")]

For the 2020–21 season, all of the technical elements in any figure skating performance – such as [jumps](/wiki/Figure_skating_jumps "Figure skating jumps"), [spins](/wiki/Figure_skating_spins "Figure skating spins"), and [lifts](/wiki/Figure_skating_lifts "Figure skating lifts") – were assigned a predetermined base point value and were then scored by a panel of nine judges on a scale from −5 to +5 based on their quality of execution.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInternational_Skating_Union202177–78-79) The judging panel's Grade of Execution (GOE) was determined by calculating the [trimmed mean](/wiki/Mean#Truncated_mean "Mean") (that is, an average after deleting the highest and lowest scores), and this GOE was added to the base value to come up with the final score for each element. The panel's scores for all elements were added together to generate a total element score.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInternational_Skating_Union202115–16-80) At the same time, judges evaluated each performance based on five program components – skating skills, transitions, performance/execution, composition, and interpretation – and assigned a score from .25 to 10 in .25 point increments. The judging panel's final score for each program component was also determined by calculating the trimmed mean. Those scores were then multiplied by the factor shown on the following chart; the results were added together to generate a total program component score.[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInternational_Skating_Union202116–17-81)

Program component factoring[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInternational_Skating_Union202116–17-81)

| Discipline | [Short program](/wiki/Short_program_(figure_skating) "Short program (figure skating)") **or** [Rhythm dance](/wiki/Rhythm_dance "Rhythm dance") | [Free skate](/wiki/Free_skating "Free skating") **or** [Free dance](/wiki/Free_dance_(ice_dance) "Free dance (ice dance)") |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Men | 1.00 | 2.00 |
| Women | 0.80 | 1.60 |
| Pairs | 0.80 | 1.60 |
| Ice dance | 0.80 | 1.20 |

Deductions were applied for certain violations like time infractions, stops and restarts, or falls.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInternational_Skating_Union202118–19-82) The total element score and total program component score were added together, minus any deductions, to generate a final performance score for each skater or team.[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInternational_Skating_Union202120-83)

## Results

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2021_World_Figure_Skating_Championships&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Results")]

### Men's singles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2021_World_Figure_Skating_Championships&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Men's singles")]

[Nathan Chen](/wiki/Nathan_Chen "Nathan Chen") of the United States, who had been in third place after the [short program](/wiki/Short_program_(figure_skating) "Short program (figure skating)"), rallied back in the [free skate](/wiki/Free_skating "Free skating"), successfully performing five [quadruple jumps](/wiki/Quadruple_jump "Quadruple jump") and finishing with an almost 30-point lead over the silver medalist, [Yuma Kagiyama](/wiki/Yuma_Kagiyama "Yuma Kagiyama") of Japan. This was Chen's third World Championship win in a row; he was the first man to win three titles in a row since [Patrick Chan](/wiki/Patrick_Chan "Patrick Chan") of Canada (2011‍–‍2013), and the first American skater since [Scott Hamilton](/wiki/Scott_Hamilton_(figure_skater) "Scott Hamilton (figure skater)") (1982‍–‍1984).[[85]](#cite_note-85) Chen had not lost an international event since finishing fifth at the [2018 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_2018_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_singles "Figure skating at the 2018 Winter Olympics – Men's singles"). In a statement to the media, Chen said, "The fact that I'm able to be here at this world championship after that unprecedented year... I'm very elated right now."[[86]](#cite_note-86)

[Yuzuru Hanyu](/wiki/Yuzuru_Hanyu "Yuzuru Hanyu") of Japan, two-time Olympic champion and two-time World champion, was in the lead after the short program, but an error on his [quadruple loop](/wiki/Loop_jump "Loop jump") and stumbles on his [triple Axels](/wiki/Axel_jump "Axel jump") caused him to finish the competition in third place. It was Hanyu's eighth World Championship medal since 2012.[[87]](#cite_note-87)

On the other hand, [Vincent Zhou](/wiki/Vincent_Zhou "Vincent Zhou") of the United States, who won the bronze medal at the [2019 World Championships](/wiki/2019_World_Figure_Skating_Championships "2019 World Figure Skating Championships"), had a disastrous performance during his short program, failing to land all three of his [jumping elements](/wiki/Figure_skating_jumps "Figure skating jumps"). He finished in 25th place, missing the cut-off for the free skate. "It's always better to talk about it than drown in my own misery," Zhou stated afterward. "To me, it feels like I let down my teammates and myself, but dwelling on those things is only going to make me feel worse."[[88]](#cite_note-88)

### Women's singles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2021_World_Figure_Skating_Championships&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Women's singles")]

[Yi Christy Leung](/wiki/Yi_Christy_Leung "Yi Christy Leung") of Hong Kong withdrew prior to the [short program](/wiki/Short_program_(figure_skating) "Short program (figure skating)") due to an injury.[[90]](#cite_note-90) Likewise, [Maé-Bérénice Méité](/wiki/Ma%C3%A9-B%C3%A9r%C3%A9nice_M%C3%A9it%C3%A9 "Maé-Bérénice Méité") of France suffered an [Achilles tendon rupture](/wiki/Achilles_tendon_rupture "Achilles tendon rupture") from a fall during her short program and withdrew from the competition.[[91]](#cite_note-91)

[Anna Shcherbakova](/wiki/Anna_Shcherbakova "Anna Shcherbakova"), representing the [Figure Skating Federation of Russia](/wiki/Figure_Skating_Federation_of_Russia "Figure Skating Federation of Russia"), won the gold medal, while her teammates [Elizaveta Tuktamysheva](/wiki/Elizaveta_Tuktamysheva "Elizaveta Tuktamysheva") and [Alexandra Trusova](/wiki/Alexandra_Trusova "Alexandra Trusova") finished second and third, respectively. Trusova had been in twelfth place after the short program, but rallied back with the highest score in the [free skating](/wiki/Free_skating "Free skating") to win the bronze medal. It was the first [podium sweep](/wiki/Podium_sweep "Podium sweep") in the women's event at the World Championships since [1991](/wiki/1991_World_Figure_Skating_Championships "1991 World Figure Skating Championships"), when skaters from the United States captured the top three spots.[[92]](#cite_note-92)

[Karen Chen](/wiki/Karen_Chen "Karen Chen") of the United States finished in fourth place, performing an "inspired program" and securing three spots for the United States in the women's event at the [2022 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_2022_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_singles "Figure skating at the 2022 Winter Olympics – Women's singles"). This was a near-repeat of Chen's performance at the [2017 World Championships](/wiki/2017_World_Figure_Skating_Championships "2017 World Figure Skating Championships"), where she had also finished fourth, securing three spots for the United States at the [2018 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_2018_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Ladies%27_singles "Figure skating at the 2018 Winter Olympics – Ladies' singles").[[93]](#cite_note-93)

### Pairs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2021_World_Figure_Skating_Championships&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Pairs")]

[Anastasia Mishina](/wiki/Anastasia_Mishina "Anastasia Mishina") and [Aleksandr Galliamov](/wiki/Aleksandr_Galliamov "Aleksandr Galliamov"), representing the [Figure Skating Federation of Russia](/wiki/Figure_Skating_Federation_of_Russia "Figure Skating Federation of Russia"), won the gold medal at their first World Championship competition after winning the [free skating](/wiki/Free_skating "Free skating") segment with their performance to the [Queen](/wiki/Queen_(band) "Queen (band)") songs "[Bohemian Rhapsody](/wiki/Bohemian_Rhapsody "Bohemian Rhapsody")" and "[We Are the Champions](/wiki/We_Are_the_Champions "We Are the Champions")".[[95]](#cite_note-95) Mishina and Galliamov had won the World Junior Championships just [two years earlier](/wiki/2019_World_Junior_Figure_Skating_Championships "2019 World Junior Figure Skating Championships"). They were the first pairs team to win the World Championships in their first appearance since [Ekaterina Gordeeva](/wiki/Ekaterina_Gordeeva "Ekaterina Gordeeva") and [Sergei Grinkov](/wiki/Sergei_Grinkov "Sergei Grinkov") in [1986](/wiki/1986_World_Figure_Skating_Championships "1986 World Figure Skating Championships").[[96]](#cite_note-96)

[Sui Wenjing](/wiki/Sui_Wenjing "Sui Wenjing") and [Han Cong](/wiki/Han_Cong "Han Cong") of China, [2018 Winter Olympic](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_2018_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Pair_skating "Figure skating at the 2018 Winter Olympics – Pair skating") silver medalists and two-time World Champions, finished in second place. Due to injury, Sui and Han had only been able to practice on ice together for two months. Meanwhile, [Aleksandra Boikova](/wiki/Aleksandra_Boikova "Aleksandra Boikova") and [Dmitrii Kozlovskii](/wiki/Dmitrii_Kozlovskii "Dmitrii Kozlovskii") of Russia, who were in the lead after the [short program](/wiki/Short_program_(figure_skating) "Short program (figure skating)"), finished in third place.[[97]](#cite_note-97)

### Ice dance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2021_World_Figure_Skating_Championships&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Ice dance")]

[Tina Garabedian](/wiki/Tina_Garabedian "Tina Garabedian") and [Simon Proulx-Sénécal](/wiki/Simon_Proulx-S%C3%A9n%C3%A9cal "Simon Proulx-Sénécal") of Armenia withdrew prior to the [rhythm dance](/wiki/Rhythm_dance "Rhythm dance") for an unspecified medical reason, which was later revealed to be a false positive COVID-19 test from Proulx-Sénécal.[[99]](#cite_note-99)[[44]](#cite_note-Proulx-Sénécal-44)

[Victoria Sinitsina](/wiki/Victoria_Sinitsina "Victoria Sinitsina") and [Nikita Katsalapov](/wiki/Nikita_Katsalapov "Nikita Katsalapov"), representing the [Figure Skating Federation of Russia](/wiki/Figure_Skating_Federation_of_Russia "Figure Skating Federation of Russia"), won both the rhythm dance and [free dance](/wiki/Free_dance "Free dance") segments, including a personal best score in the free dance, to win the World Championship title. They were the first Russian team to win a World Championship title in ice dance since [Oksana Domnina](/wiki/Oksana_Domnina "Oksana Domnina") and [Maxim Shabalin](/wiki/Maxim_Shabalin "Maxim Shabalin") in [2009](/wiki/2009_World_Figure_Skating_Championships "2009 World Figure Skating Championships").[[77]](#cite_note-ID-2021-77) [Madison Hubbell](/wiki/Madison_Hubbell "Madison Hubbell") and [Zachary Donohue](/wiki/Zachary_Donohue "Zachary Donohue") of the United States finished in second place with a "captivating program."[[100]](#cite_note-USA03272021-100) "It is really a joy to skate together," Hubbell stated afterward, "but there is also that little dissatisfaction of not reaching our goal of bringing home the gold medal."[[100]](#cite_note-USA03272021-100) Likewise, their teammates [Madison Chock](/wiki/Madison_Chock "Madison Chock") and [Evan Bates](/wiki/Evan_Bates "Evan Bates") had hoped to win a third world medal with their iconic [snake charming](/wiki/Snake_charming "Snake charming") program, but finished in fourth place after Bates made a minor error on his [twizzles](/wiki/Twizzle "Twizzle"). Still, their high performance, along with that of Hubbell and Donohue, netted the United States three quota spots in ice dance at the [2022 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_2022_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Ice_dance "Figure skating at the 2022 Winter Olympics – Ice dance").[[101]](#cite_note-ISU-2388-101)

[Piper Gilles](/wiki/Piper_Gilles "Piper Gilles") and [Paul Poirier](/wiki/Paul_Poirier "Paul Poirier") of Canada, who had hoped to win a World Championship medal in [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal"), which had been scheduled to host the [2020 World Championships](/wiki/2020_World_Figure_Skating_Championships "2020 World Figure Skating Championships") before it was cancelled due to the COVID-19 pandemic, finished in third place. Their total score of 214.35 was a new personal best. "I think we're absolutely thrilled with what we did today," Gilles said afterward. "We just love to perform and skate and I think that came out on the ice today."[[102]](#cite_note-102)

## Olympic qualifying results

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2021_World_Figure_Skating_Championships&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Olympic qualifying results")]

Main article: [Figure skating at the 2022 Winter Olympics – Qualification](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_2022_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Qualification "Figure skating at the 2022 Winter Olympics – Qualification")

The results of the 2021 World Championships determined 82 spots for the [2022 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_2022_Winter_Olympics "Figure skating at the 2022 Winter Olympics"): 23 entries in the men's event, 24 in the women's event, 16 in the pairs event, and 19 in the ice dance event. One allocated spot in men's singles was unused due to an insufficient number of member nations meeting the criteria, and the spot was re-allocated to the next qualifying event: the [2021 Nebelhorn Trophy](/wiki/2021_CS_Nebelhorn_Trophy "2021 CS Nebelhorn Trophy").[[101]](#cite_note-ISU-2388-101)

Athletes from Russia would compete under the [Russian Olympic Committee](/wiki/Russian_Olympic_Committee "Russian Olympic Committee") (ROC) flag at the [2022 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2022_Winter_Olympics "2022 Winter Olympics").[[104]](#cite_note-104)
